data.title: The model bought from Huading may be fake: Unveiling the gray market chain of AI transfer stations
data.description:
You think you're coding with Claude Opus 4.6, but in the background it might be a low-end, 9-parameter domestic model. You think you're saving money, but every prompt you make is being archived and used to train competing models. You think you've found a replacement, but the money on your bill is flowing into a gray market industry that starts with credit card fraud. This isn't a conspiracy theory. An arXiv paper uses data to prove that 45.83% of the "top-tier models" you spent real money tuning fail authentication. Even more frightening is that this is hardly a secret within the industry. Bonus at the end: a community-verified 30-second quick detection method. First, let's clarify: What exactly is an AI transit station? On July 9, 2024, OpenAI officially cut off API services to mainland China and Hong Kong. In September 2025, Anthropic followed suit, completely banning Chinese-controlled companies from using the Claude API. Google's Gemini also imposes strict restrictions on Chinese IP addresses. For Chinese developers, the doors to directly using the world's top AI models have closed one after another. Thus, the "transfer station" came into being. In short, a transit station is essentially an intermediary agent—it claims to help you bypass regional restrictions and payment barriers, allowing you to call the APIs of models like Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini at a lower price. You only need to replace a base_url and API key; without changing a single line of code, you can "seamlessly access" the world's most powerful AI models. This sounds wonderful. But behind this "wonderful" facade lies a pitfall you can't imagine. What does a legitimate transit station look like? Let's take a look at OpenRouter. Before discussing the dark side, it's necessary to examine how a "legitimate transit station" does business, so you can see the vast difference. OpenRouter is currently the world's largest AI model aggregation platform, connecting over 300 models and more than 60 providers. Its business model is extremely transparent: it charges approximately 5% service fee on top of the official inference fee (customized solutions are available for large clients). Every penny you pay has a clear destination—the model call fee goes to the upstream vendor, and the difference goes to OpenRouter. In 2025, the company secured $40 million in Series A funding led by a16z and Menlo Ventures, valuing the company at $500 million, with an ARR of $5 million, representing a 400% year-over-year growth. Its core selling point is "routing"—one API key connects to all models, enabling intelligent failover, and with transparent pricing. If you call Opus 4.6, you get Opus 4.6. Similar legitimate channels include EdenAI and Azure OpenAI Service, which have formal commercial partnerships with model vendors and are subject to compliance regulations. However, a problem arises—OpenRouter began imposing "account-level" bans on Chinese users at the end of 2025, restricting the use of models from the OpenAI, Claude, and Google platforms. Legitimate channels are becoming increasingly limited for Chinese users. This has precisely fueled the rampant growth of "underground relay stations." Dissecting the Four-Layer Gray Industry Chain of Relay Stations Domestic AI relay stations are far more complex than simply "proxy forwarding." This has formed an extremely sophisticated gray industry chain. The low prices you see are just the tip of the iceberg—what lies beneath the surface is far dirtier than you imagine. At the very bottom: Credit card fraud. At the darkest level of this industry chain lies credit card fraud. Some people possess large numbers of overseas black market credit card numbers, using platforms like OpenAI and Anthropic to create accounts in bulk and obtain API credit limits without requiring real-name registration overseas. The actual cost of these accounts is close to zero—because the money is deducted from the stolen credit cards. When you cheer for prices "as low as 30% of the official price," have you ever wondered—how can this price be achieved? This isn't efficiency optimization, nor is it economies of scale; it's someone else "paying the bill" for you—that "someone" might be a victim of credit card fraud. The second layer: Web-based reverse engineering—the business of converting subscriptions to APIs. Slightly more "respectable" than fraud is Web2API reverse engineering—cracking web-based subscription services into API interfaces for sale. These intermediary sites don't use official APIs; instead, they reverse-engineer the web-based interaction protocols of products like Claude and ChatGPT, capture and parse the session authentication process, and encapsulate the web-based calls into pseudo-APIs compatible with OpenAI format. The specific operation is as follows: Register Plus/Pro member accounts in batches to build an "account pool," and then use a proxy server for load balancing to distribute user requests across different accounts. A ChatGPT Plus account, costing $20 per month, can be shared by 5 to 20 people, each paying only a few dollars. Moreover, all of this is supported by a mature open-source toolchain. One API (GitHub 31.2k stars) is currently the most mainstream API aggregation and management tool, supporting unified access to over 30 large models, providing a full suite of functions including load balancing, token management, and channel management, one-click Docker deployment, and the MIT open-source license. The New API (GitHub 24k stars) is a secondary development based on the One API, adding commercial features such as online payment, intelligent channel routing, and cache billing, and adopts the AGPL-3.0 license. More recently, Sub2API (GitHub 9.5k stars) has become even more popular. The project's name translates directly to "Subscription to API"—it specifically converts subscription accounts for products like Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini into API interfaces. The project supports multi-account management, intelligent scheduling, session persistence, concurrency control, and even a complete management dashboard. The project's README contains a small, honest statement: "Using this project may violate Anthropic's Terms of Service. All risks are borne by the user." These three projects combined have over 64,000 stars, forming a complete "transfer station infrastructure." Anyone can set up a fully functional API transfer service in hours—deployment tutorials are everywhere, and ads for "zero-threshold, monthly income exceeding 10,000 yuan" side hustles are ubiquitous in developer communities. The third layer: Industrialized harvesting of free quotas. The free trial quotas offered by AI vendors to new users have also been targeted by black market actors. Taking Cursor as an example, multiple open-source projects on GitHub achieve unlimited free trial quotas by resetting device fingerprints. These projects have received thousands of stars, forming a complete closed loop of "open-source tools attracting users, paid accounts generating revenue." Manus AI's invitation points system was also compromised—automatic registration scripts developed by black market operators are priced between 1580 and 3200 yuan, reducing the cost of acquiring points to "3300 points for only 0.5 yuan." More than 125 related fraudulent products appeared on e-commerce platforms at one point. The Fourth Layer: The "Legitimate" Resellers in Suits There's another type of reseller that takes a seemingly "compliant" route—claiming to reduce costs through large-scale procurement and resell API quotas at a discount lower than the official price. Some even claim "1 yuan = 1 dollar"—for an official API quota of $1, the reseller only charges 1 yuan, equivalent to one-seventh of the official price. But where does the discount come from? There are only a few possibilities: either the model has been compromised, or they're using the "cheap supply" from the previous three layers, or they're initially using low prices to attract users and burn money, then figuring out how to monetize once they've built a large user base—or they might simply disappear. When you see a product priced far below cost, remember this: if you can't find anyone else paying for it, you are. If the above is just "industry rumors," then the following section contains irrefutable academic evidence. In March 2026, a paper titled "Real Money, Fake Models: Deceptive Model Claims in Shadow APIs" was published on arXiv (paper number 2603.01919), providing the first systematic academic audit of AI intermediaries. The research team identified 17 Shadow API services and found that 187 academic papers used these intermediaries. They then conducted in-depth analysis of three representative services. The findings are alarming: 45.83% of the model endpoints failed identity fingerprint verification. Nearly half. The model you're calling and the one you think you're using are very likely not the same thing. The paper categorizes fraudulent methods into three types: "Substitution Type"—This type claims to provide a certain version of the Gemini model, but actually replaces it with another version. The fingerprint verification result is completely inconsistent with the claimed model identity, yet the seller charges a premium of up to 7 times the original price. "Hypocrisy Type"—This is the most outrageous. A user calls Claude Opus 4.6 (GPT-5 in the paper's example), and the price appears to be the same as the official price, but the actual returned model is GLM-4-9B—a small, open-source model with a completely different number of parameters and capabilities. You spend tens of dollars per million tokens, only to receive the output of a model that runs almost for free. "Resale for Profit" – This involves purchasing weak model calls cheaply from upstream suppliers, repackaging them as top-tier models, and selling them to profit from the price difference. The paper presents stark data: Users pay 100% of the official price, but actually receive only 38% to 52% of the model's value. In monetary terms: for every $14.84 spent, the actual service you receive is only worth $5.70 to $7.77; the rest goes into the pockets of the middleman. Even more dangerous is performance collapse. In MedQA (Medical Q&A) benchmarks, the performance of the Gemini-2.5-flash provided by the intermediary platform plummeted from the official 83.82% to 37.00%—a drop of 46 percentage points. The gap in LegalBench was as high as 40 to 43 percentage points. In Mathematical Reasoning (AIME 2025), the deviation reached 40 percentage points. Imagine: medical consultation code written using this "intermediary Opus," legal analysis run using this "intermediary GPT-5," and academic papers submitted using this "intermediary Claude"—their reliability might be less than that of a free small model. It is estimated that approximately 56 academic studies required redoing due to citations of the Shadow API, involving costs ranging from $115,000 to $140,000. The conclusion is straightforward: the Shadow API should not be used in any scenario requiring reliability. The paper reveals the severity of the problem. But for ordinary developers, a more pressing question is—is the relay station I'm using actually legitimate? Is your model genuine? Community Practical Detection Manual Since spoofing is so prevalent, is there a way for ordinary users to verify it themselves? The paper and the technical community provide a complete set of methods, from "instant testing" to "professional auditing." The following detection methods come from highly-rated practice posts and open-source tools in the X (Twitter) developer community and have been verified by a large number of users. Method Zero: 30-Second Quick Screening (Temperature Set to 0.01) This is the most widely circulated "demon-revealing" test in the community, a highly praised post from @billtheinvestor: Input this string of numbers: "5, 15, 77, 19, 53, 54", and let the model sort or select the maximum value. True Claude: Almost always gets 77. True GPT-5.4: Often gets 162 (add the numbers). If the results fluctuate wildly after 10 consecutive tests, the probability of it being fake is extremely high. The principle is simple: different models have different training data and fine-tuning styles for instructions, and will have fixed "behavioral fingerprints" when faced with such fuzzy instructions. Fake models will either answer incorrectly or give different answers every time. Auxiliary Check 1: Abnormal Token Consumption

Send a simple "ping" (e.g., just type "hi") and check the returned input_tokens. If it shows more than 200 tokens—90% likely fake. This means the intermediate layer is stuffing you with a huge number of hidden system prompts to override your commands.

Auxiliary Check 1: Abnormal Token Consumption

Send a simple "ping" (e.g., just type "hi") and check the returned input_tokens. If it shows more than 200 tokens—90% likely fake.

. This means the intermediate layer is stuffing you with a huge number of hidden system prompts to override your commands. Auxiliary Check 2: Rejection Style Judgment Ask a question that violates the rules (e.g., "How to make a bomb") and observe the rejection language: Real Claude: Polite but firm, "Sorry but I can't assist with that." Fake Model/Local Mini-Model: Frequently uses emojis, verbose tone, even saying "Sorry, Master~" Auxiliary Check 3: Functional Missing Point Check If the transfer station claims to be Opus 4.6/GPT-5.4, but: Function calling is not supported; image recognition is not possible; long contexts (e.g., 32k) are unstable. → This is most likely a weak model impersonating another. Method 1: Directly "interrogate" the model's identity. Although system prompts can forge identity claims, many low-quality intermediaries won't go this far. Directly ask "What model are you?" or "Please describe your training data deadline." If a model claiming to be Opus 4.6 gets its own basic information wrong, there's a high chance something's wrong. Method Two: Latency and Token Fluctuation Analysis The official API's inference latency and token count are relatively stable. However, if you find that the response time for the same question fluctuates wildly, or the output length fluctuates abnormally, it may mean that the backend model is being frequently switched—sometimes given a genuine model, sometimes a cheaper one. Send the same prompt repeatedly more than 10 times and observe the consistency of the response time and output content. Method Three: Capability Boundary Testing The difference between top-level models and smaller models is most obvious in complex inference tasks. Prepare several challenging math problems, logic puzzles, or professional domain problems with clear answers (such as AIME competition problems), and send the same request to both official channels and intermediary platforms to compare the quality of the answers. If a model claiming to be Opus 4.6 frequently fails on basic reasoning problems, it's likely not genuine. Method Four: LLMmap Fingerprint Recognition (Professional Level) This is the core method used in the paper—LLMmap is an active fingerprint recognition framework that sends 3 to 8 carefully designed queries to the model, analyzes the statistical characteristics of the responses (word frequency, sentence structure, specific expression habits), and calculates the cosine distance with a known model fingerprint database. Even if the model is disguised, this method can penetrate the disguise. In short: If a relay station is hesitant to let you run any of the above tests, or if the test results don't match the official ones—run them, and don't look back. Small-scale testing and recharging only after use is the most pragmatic self-preservation strategy at this stage. Every prompt you send is being sold for a price. If model manipulation is "giving you less," then data selling is "taking more from you." The technical essence of a relay station is a proxy layer—every prompt and every response you send passes completely through its server. The relay station operator can effortlessly obtain all the code, business plans, customer data, and private conversations you send. This is not a theoretical deduction. Extensive discussions within the developer community have long pointed out that it's an open secret that relay stations use user request data for model distillation. Model distillation, simply put, is using the output of a large model to train a smaller model—a technique of "stealing" skills. All requests that pass through the relay—the complete prompt and response—constitute a ready-made, high-quality training dataset. Especially the outputs of top-tier models like Opus 4.6 and GPT-5, which are themselves extremely valuable distillation corpora. In early 2026, Anthropic released a report directly accusing three Chinese AI labs—DeepSeek, Moonshot AI, and MiniMax—of using a network of fake accounts to massively access the Claude API for model distillation. MiniMax had over 13 million interactions, and Moonshot over 3.4 million. Their "Hydra cluster" architecture—a network of numerous fake accounts—is identical to the "account pool" model of relay stations. From a technical architecture perspective, relay stations are divided into "pure pass-through" (requests are forwarded in real-time, without disk write-to-disk) and "store-and-forward" (data is stored first, then forwarded). However, even for services claiming to be "pure pass-through," no one can audit whether data is actually stored in the backend. Your trust is entirely based on the verbal promises of an anonymous operator. Security experts recommend evaluating transit stations from five dimensions: whether the technical architecture is transparent, whether the logging policy only records billing metadata, whether transmission uses TLS 1.2+, whether the API key is completely isolated, and whether there is a leak emergency mechanism. However, in reality, the vast majority of domestic transit stations are not even transparent about their main information, let alone accept independent security audits. Relay stations also face a fatal systemic risk—running away. Most transit stations use a pre-paid model, where you pay first and then are charged based on usage. Once the operator disappears, your balance vanishes completely, and you have no way to hold them accountable. HodlAI is a textbook example: Initially, the project team generously offered low-priced APIs to attract users to deposit funds. However, when the national treasury funds dwindled to approximately $60,000 and daily token consumption reached $10,000, they began drastically tightening restrictions—capping single requests at 50,000 tokens and escalating frequency limits. When users questioned this in a Telegram group, they were immediately kicked out of the group and their accounts banned. Community comments were succinct: "Just like a pyramid scheme," "Keeping quiet is far easier than solving problems," and "The same old formula, the same old taste." Insiders summarize this model in one sentence: "First, attract users with low prices; once the user base is large, the upstream accounts are blocked, and they simply disappear. The only ones who suffer are the users." Similar posts seeking redress are commonplace in developer communities like Linux.do and V2EX. Some of these intermediary sites have extremely unfair contract terms, and some don't even have any business registration information. You don't even know who to sue. A complete industry chain: From black market SIM cards to your IDE. Putting all the above information together, you'll see a clear chain: Upstream ammunition—SMS verification platforms provide phone numbers, black market SIM card providers provide payment methods, and SIM card pools provide device resources. Midstream weapons—Reverse engineers crack protocols, open-source projects like One API/New API/Sub2API provide ready-made infrastructure, and device farms mass-produce accounts. Downstream distribution—Relay station operators package it as "API services" for sale, Telegram groups and e-commerce platforms become sales channels, and some even package "building a relay station" as a side hustle training course. And you—through IDE tools like Cursor and Claude Code, or through your own code—are the end consumer in this chain. Data from the security company Threat Hunter shows that of the 50 AI Agent products they sampled, every single one has black market derivative services. This industry chain has evolved from account trading in 2022, to API resale in 2023, to free quota arbitrage in 2024, to Agent computing power abuse in 2025, and by 2026—it has completed a full evolution from small-scale workshops to industrialized production. In conclusion, the story of the AI ​​transfer station is essentially an AI-era version of an old business logic—when you don't know what the product is, you are the product. Your money bought a fake model, your data was fed to someone else's training set, and your prepaid balance could go to zero at any time. These three things are not "possible," but "actually happening." A few practical suggestions—use official APIs whenever possible. Official APIs are expensive, but the price is justified. If your business has any requirements for data security and model reliability, a relay station should not be in your technology stack. At least learn self-testing. If you are using a relay station, run a test using the method described above. Compare the relay station's output with the official output using the same AIME math problem and the same complex code. If the difference is significant—you know what to do. Sensitive data should never pass through a relay station. If you must use it, at least do the following: de-identify sensitive information, rotate API keys regularly, and do not store any core data in the relay station account. Carefully examine domestically produced models. DeepSeek, Qwen, GLM, and other domestic modelers are rapidly catching up in terms of capabilities, with transparent pricing and prices far lower than overseas models. Their official APIs can be used legally and directly in China. Instead of risking using adulterated overseas models in a gray area, it's better to use these legitimate domestic alternatives—at least you know what you're adjusting. This industry is changing every day. But one ironclad rule remains unchanged: Choosing the cheapest option without understanding the costs is often the most expensive decision.

data.title: From Guessing Games to Temporary Rules: A Decade of Absurdity in Crypto Regulation
data.description: On March 17th, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) released the long-awaited rulebook for the cryptocurrency industry since 2013. I am pleased with this and am working towards it. Bitcoin has fallen 44% from its October high. Ethereum is priced around $2,000, less than half its price seven months ago. The total market capitalization of altcoins has evaporated $470 billion since its peak. The Fear & Greed Index has reached 11. This isn't just a bad week; it's 11 out of 100. This means people have stopped arguing about where the bottom is and have started selling off their remaining cryptocurrencies. And just then, on March 17th, the SEC and CFTC released a document that finally reveals exactly what the tokens you hold are. Previously, the two sides had endured a decade-long legal battle, hundreds of enforcement actions, and billions of dollars in legal fees. Some companies even chose to relocate to Singapore rather than continue playing a guessing game with Gary Gensler. The answer was finally revealed the week Ethereum's price fell below $1900. But the key point is that while the token economy itself suffered a major blow, everything beneath it is thriving. The circulating supply of stablecoins has surpassed $316 billion, and the size of on-chain real-world assets (RWA) has reached $26.5 billion and continues to grow. As a result, Morgan Stanley is building a crypto trust bank. Meta abandoned its Metaverse project but is bringing stablecoins to WhatsApp. Stripe is processing $400 billion worth of stablecoin transactions. Nasdaq is building a tokenized stock trading platform. Cryptocurrencies are becoming a pillar of global finance, and in most cases, it doesn't rely on tokens. Cryptocurrencies are no longer merely a speculative asset class. The regulations issued on March 17th were originally designed for first-generation cryptocurrencies, but they were only formally implemented after the advent of the second generation of cryptocurrencies. However, this doesn't mean they are meaningless. As SEC Chairman Paul Atkins once said: "We are no longer the 'Securities and Everything Commission'." "Is this a bit late?" For the first time, US regulators have provided a unified definition of cryptocurrencies. Five categories, with each token falling into one. I will present these definitions below; please read with the assumption that you've never heard of these concepts before. Digital commodities are the main focus. A digital commodity is a crypto asset whose value derives from the programmed operation of a fully functional crypto system and supply and demand dynamics. Its value does not depend on the management of a central issuing authority. If the network is truly decentralized and functioning properly, without any single company backing it, then the asset is a commodity. This is governed by the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), not the US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). Sixteen mainstream tokens, including Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana, XRP, Cardano, Avalanche, Polkadot, Chainlink, Dogecoin, and Shiba Inu Coin, have been officially recognized as digital goods. Dogecoin and Shiba Inu Coin meet this definition because no initiator or institution is driving their value growth. They lack promises, roadmaps, and ongoing team work crucial to their token value. This is why they are considered goods rather than securities. The criterion is whether someone promises a return based on their work. Digital securities refer to tokenized stocks, bonds, and government bonds. Simply put, these assets are securities before being put on the blockchain and remain securities afterward. The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) regulates these assets. It's that simple. Digital collectibles are NFTs tied to specific items or experiences. Digital tools are assets used to access software or services without expecting a return on investment. Stablecoins have their own category under the GENIUS Act. Staking, mining, and airdrops have all been approved. The ruling explicitly states that receiving mining rewards, participating in on-chain staking, or receiving digital goods airdrops do not constitute securities trading. This eliminates one of the biggest legal risks faced by proof-of-stake networks since the Gensler era. Packaging non-security tokens has also been approved. These 16 named tokens are all underlying infrastructure, backed by years of decentralized development. DeFi protocol tokens—such as JUP, POL, METEOR, and most tokens launched in the last two years—were not named and clearly do not meet the criteria. A fully functional crypto system without centralized regulatory oversight is a high bar. Most actively developed protocols do not meet this standard. This gray area, which this interpretation should address, remains unclear for the tokens actually held by most people. Value must derive from the programmatic operation of a fully functional system, not from someone's promise. This testing standard transforms a decade-long ambiguity into something compliance officers can actually tackle. There's more to this than meets the eye. This announcement does not constitute a formal rulemaking process under the Administrative Procedure Act, nor is it legally binding as a law or officially promulgated regulation. You'd better read that sentence again. This 68-page document we've been waiting for is merely an interpretative announcement, not a law or regulation, but simply an institutional position statement issued by the current chairmen of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), which they can withdraw at any time. This interpretation is a formal institutional action by the SEC and CFTC and is legally binding. However, in the absence of relevant legislation, future administrations can amend it. The document itself reserves the right for each agency to refine or expand its views. A future SEC chair with a differing political stance can overturn this interpretation without congressional approval. The next administration doesn't even need new laws, just new leadership. Atkins was well aware of this. He expressed this view on the day it was released, calling on Congress to take action to provide more lasting clarity. He saw this interpretation as a transitional measure, awaiting Congress to take action on comprehensive market structure legislation. This legislation is the Market Structure Transparency Act (CLARITY Act). Currently, the CLARITY Act is being considered in the Senate. The House of Representatives passed the CLARITY Act in July 2025 with 294 votes. Such high support from both parties indicates a genuine consensus. Then it went to the Senate and stalled. The key obstacle to the bill's passage is the yield of stablecoins. Banks believe that allowing crypto platforms to pay interest on stablecoin balances will trigger deposit outflows. People will withdraw money from their savings accounts and deposit it into USDC. To obtain higher returns, banking lobbying groups immediately began lobbying. The Senate Banking Committee canceled its planned January 2026 deliberations. The bill made no progress for the next two months. On March 20, Senators Tom Tillis and Angela Alsobrooks confirmed an agreement in principle on stablecoin rewards, which was supported by the White House. The agreement prohibited passive yields on stablecoins; rewards for activities linked to payments and platform usage were still allowed. Neither side was satisfied, and compromises are often made this way. But the yield agreement was only one of five things that needed to be completed before the CLARITY Act could take effect. The completion of the other four legislative steps came at a time of great tension this year. The Senate Banking Committee deliberations; and a full Senate vote (requiring 60 votes) Coordination with the Agriculture Committee and with the House version. Presidential signature. The Banking Committee's deliberations are scheduled for the second half of April, after the Easter recess. Senator Bernie Moreno warned that if the bill fails to reach the full Senate before May, digital asset legislation may not make progress for years. In addition, the Iran war also occupied a significant amount of Senate discussion time. There was also the voter identification bill that Trump indicated he wanted to pass first. Provisions regarding decentralized finance (DeFi) remain unresolved, with Senate Democrats expressing concern about the potential for illicit financial activities. Ethics provisions are also yet to be finalized, particularly whether senior government officials should be prohibited from profiting from crypto assets—a politically sensitive issue given the current administration's holdings of cryptocurrency. Senate Republicans are currently discussing attaching deregulation provisions for community banks as a political bargaining chip to the bill, which would trigger a new round of negotiations. The U.S. House Financial Services Committee recently held a hearing entitled "Tokenization and the Future of Securities: Modernizing Capital Markets." Witnesses included Kenneth Bentsen of the Securities Industry and Financial Markets Association (SIFMA), Summer Mersinger of the Blockchain Association, Christian Sabella of the DTCC, and John Zeka of Nasdaq. Zecca). Nasdaq and the New York Stock Exchange are both building tokenized stock trading platforms. DTCC handles the current settlement. If the DTCC endorses the efficiency of blockchain, then this debate is effectively over. Therefore, infrastructure construction is based on a rulebook that may not exist in two years. This is the dilemma the industry currently faces. Companies are making billions of dollars in decisions to build custody systems, tokenized platforms, and staking infrastructure, all based on a persuasive but not legally binding interpretative document. What is eternal, and what is not? For readers holding the aforementioned 16 tokens (such as ETH, SOL, XRP), these tokens are now officially recognized as digital goods under US law due to statements from two regulatory officials. This classification will remain in effect as long as these two officials or their successors maintain this designation. If CLARITY Once the bill is passed, it becomes law. No future president has the power to overturn it without congressional approval. The listed assets will be permanently defined, and the classification criteria will be binding. If it hasn't passed by May, the current classification system will depend on the opinion of a single government department. Currently, 16 named assets are temporarily safe, but not all assets are named. Most decentralized finance (DeFi), most new tokens, and any permissionless asset without a clearly defined issuer remain in a gray area, an issue not explicitly addressed in previous interpretations. The most anticipated sentence is like a pencil draft. Someone needs to pick up a pen and formally implement this. Everything depends on the Senate's actions over the next six weeks. Will these rules last long enough for all of this to make sense?
